title: Closing at Intermediate Depth
event: At year-end, Ingle Co. shows Service Revenue of $210,000, Operating Expenses of $140,000, Interest Expense of $24,000, and Dividends of $20,000. Prepare the closing entries and compute the net change in Retained Earnings.

lines.label: zeroed
lines.why: Empty the revenue account against its credit balance.
lines.trap: Temporary accounts restart at zero — that's the whole point of closing.

lines.label: revenues in
lines.trap: Income Summary never appears on any statement — it lives only inside the closing process.

lines.why: All expenses gather in one debit.
lines.trap: One combined entry — line-by-line closings burn exam time.

lines.label: zeroed
lines.trap: Credits to expenses happen exactly once a year — here.

lines.label: zeroed
lines.trap: Every expense account gets its own credit line in the combined entry.

lines.label: net income out
lines.why: The 46,000 credit balance is the year's net income, transferred out.
lines.trap: If the summary's balance doesn't equal the income statement's bottom line, something upstream broke.

lines.label: income banked
lines.trap: Direction flips in a loss year — RE would take the debit.

lines.label: dividends out
lines.trap: Dividends never pass through Income Summary — they are a distribution, not an expense.

lines.label: zeroed
lines.trap: Net RE change = 46,000 in − 20,000 out = +26,000. Verify it against the RE statement.

memorize.body: REID order: Revenues, Expenses, Income Summary, Dividends.
Net change in RE = net income − dividends. The closing entries just execute that arithmetic through the ledger.

questions.prompt: Revenue $210,000, expenses $164,000, dividends $20,000. Net CHANGE in Retained Earnings = ?

distractors.feedback: That's net income before dividends. The 20,000 distribution also hits RE: net +26,000.
You added the dividends. They REDUCE retained earnings: 46,000 − 20,000.

build.scaffold: Four entries, REID order. Entry 3 carries income; entry 4 carries the distribution — they never mix.
